title: Treating minor pig injuries
subtitle: How to clean and dress small wounds in pigs, and when to call a vet.
excerpt: Pigs in a group will occasionally fight, and pigs on outdoor systems will find things to cut themselves on. Most small wounds clean up well at home. The key is to act quickly, because pigs are curious animals and group-mates will investigate any wound, which delays healing.
sourceNotes: NADIS, pig health management factsheet. AHDB Pork, pig welfare and health guide. Veterinary Record, wound management in farm livestock.

glossaryTerms.definition: A topical antibiotic/antiseptic spray used on minor wounds in farm animals. The purple colour (typically oxytetracycline in a dye carrier) makes the wound visible from a distance, discourages other animals from investigating it, and has a mild antibacterial action. Available without prescription from agricultural merchants.

Lacerations typically around the head, ears, shoulders, and flanks caused by biting during dominance fighting. Most common when unfamiliar pigs are mixed, or when boars are introduced. Superficial cuts clean up well at home; deeper bites into the ear or muscle layer may need veterinary attention.

content.text: Most wounds you encounter in pigs will be
or abrasions from outdoor hazards. The priority is to clean the wound, protect it from investigation by other pigs, and monitor for infection.

Pigs are strong and difficult to hold still for wound treatment. A snare around the upper jaw (used correctly) restrains a pig temporarily and allows inspection. If you do not have experience with a hog snare, confine the pig in a corner or in a small pen with solid sides so it cannot move far, rather than trying to hold it by hand. Keep your movements calm and deliberate; a panicking pig will struggle more.

Flush the wound with clean water to remove visible debris. Use a dilute saline solution or dilute antiseptic wash if you have it. Do not scrub inside a wound; irrigation is more effective and less damaging than scrubbing. Pat dry with a clean cloth. Check that nothing is embedded in the wound; splinters, wire fragments, or grit may not be visible at first in a bloody wound; feel gently with a clean finger.

to cover the wound. The purple colour will deter other pigs from investigating the wound site. Reapply at the next check if the colour has been rubbed off. Check the wound at least twice daily for the first 48 hours.

Call a vet for: wounds that are deep, gaping, or showing underlying tissue; wounds over a joint; any wound that shows heat, swelling, or discharge after 48 hours; any pig that is lame, off feed, or running a temperature. Wounds on the feet or lower leg can become seriously infected very quickly in pigs kept on wet ground. If in doubt, treat early rather than waiting.
